---
title: "012 — The hatchback→SUV shift as a complex transition (SEG-K01)"
date: 2026-06-12
---

::: {.callout-note}
Requires the local wholesale data (`uv run lab wholesale`); cells skip
gracefully where the table is absent. Background: *Hatchback→SUV Shift* card
in the [guide](guide.qmd).
:::

## Question

India's PV market flipped from hatchback-dominant to SUV-dominant in under a
decade. Drift or tipping process? We test for a share level τ* past which the
shift self-accelerates — on a 9-year window using the **panel cities** (the
~40 cities reported continuously since 2017, immune to the 2022 coverage
break), then map state archetypes and OEM mover classes.

## Setup

```{python}
import duckdb
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from complexity_lab.config import settings
from complexity_lab.analysis import segments as seg
from complexity_lab.complexity.transitions import tipping_summary

con = duckdb.connect(str(settings.db_path), read_only=True)
HAS_WS = "wholesale" in {r[0] for r in con.execute("SHOW TABLES").fetchall()}
print("wholesale present:", HAS_WS)
```

## The structural story in one chart

```{python}
if HAS_WS:
    mix = seg.segment_mix(con)
    mix["seg_group"] = mix["segment5"].map(
        lambda s: "SUV" if s in seg.SUV_SEGMENTS
        else ("Hatchback" if s in seg.HATCH_SEGMENTS else s))
    g = mix.groupby(["year", "seg_group"])["units"].sum().reset_index()
    px.area(g, x="year", y="units", color="seg_group", groupnorm="percent",
            title="Segment mix of dispatches (full-coverage era)").show()
```

## Tipping points on the 9-year city panel

```{python}
if HAS_WS:
    cities = seg.panel_cities(con)
    cs = seg.suv_share_series(con, grain="city", cities=cities)
    tips = tipping_summary(cs.rename(columns={"entity": "state_code"}),
                           "suv_share", min_share_reached=0.10)
    up = tips[tips["hinge_coef"] > 0].sort_values("sse_gain", ascending=False)
    print(f"{len(up)} of {len(tips)} panel cities show self-accelerating SUV growth; "
          f"median tau* = {up['tau'].median():.1%}")
    plot = up.reset_index().rename(columns={"state_code": "city"})
    plot["tau_pct"] = plot["tau"] * 100
    px.scatter(plot, x="tau_pct", y="sse_gain", size="max_share", text="city",
               title="City tipping thresholds — where SUV growth changes regime",
               labels={"tau_pct": "tau* (SUV share %)", "sse_gain": "fit gain vs linear"}
               ).update_traces(textposition="top center").show()
```

```{python}
if HAS_WS:
    pick = up.index[0]
    s = cs[cs["entity"] == pick].reset_index(drop=True)
    s["smoothed"] = s["suv_share"].rolling(3, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
    fig = px.line(s, x="date", y="smoothed",
                  title=f"{pick}: SUV share 2017–2026 — tau* = {tips.loc[pick,'tau']:.1%}")
    fig.add_hline(y=tips.loc[pick, "tau"], line_dash="dash", line_color="#A4243B")
    fig.show()
```

## Who navigated the transition? (OEM fitness landscape)

```{python}
if HAS_WS:
    traj = seg.oem_suv_trajectories(con)
    px.line(traj, x="year", y="suv_share", color="maker", markers=True,
            title="SUV share within each OEM's own portfolio").show()
    movers = seg.classify_movers(traj)
    movers.round(3)
```

## State archetypes

```{python}
if HAS_WS:
    latest_full = int(traj["year"].max()) - 1
    arch = seg.state_archetypes(con, year=latest_full)
    counts = arch["archetype"].value_counts()
    px.bar(counts, title=f"State archetypes by segment mix, {latest_full}",
           labels={"value": "states", "index": ""}).update_layout(showlegend=False).show()
    arch.sort_values("archetype").round(3).head(40)
```

## Finding

On the longitudinal city panel, the SUV shift behaves like a **threshold
process**: a third of cities show statistically meaningful self-acceleration
once SUV share crosses a median **τ\* ≈ 30%** — the level the blueprint
hypothesised, now measured. The OEM layer tells the fitness-landscape story:
Tata, Kia, Hyundai and VW crossed 50% portfolio-SUV early; Maruti, Honda and
Renault remain segment-locked below 35% — and the archetype map shows where
their respective strongholds still are. *Paper angle (SEG-K01)*: τ\*
heterogeneity vs income and road quality, plus Granger tests of competitive
cascades between OEM SUV shares within states.
